reasoning: The AJAX handler _ajax_menu_icons_update_settings() at line 316 lacks a current_user_can() check, confirming a defense-in-depth gap. However, the claimed reachability ('any subscriber or above') is incorrect. The nonce for action 'menu-icons-settings-update' is generated exclusively via wp_nonce_field() inside _meta_box(), which is rendered only on wp-admin/nav-menus.php. WordPress core requires the 'edit_theme_options' capability (Editor role or higher) to access nav-menus.php. A subscriber-level user cannot load nav-menus.php and therefore cannot obtain the required nonce. Without the nonce, check_ajax_referer() kills the request. The localized JS data (wp_localize_script) exposes only the AJAX URL — not the nonce — so there is no frontend exposure path. The practical attack surface is limited to users who already have edit_theme_options, which is an elevated role that can directly edit menus via the same page. The missing capability check is a code quality issue (no explicit authorization gate), but it does not enable privilege escalation for low-privilege roles. Not a real high-severity finding as described.
